articles.title: Two Hours of Shouting
articles.dek: The wildest scene in the book of Acts is a stadium full of people who didn't know why they were there. It explains more about our world — and our hearts — than we'd like to admit.
articles.quote: The crowd that shouts the longest is often the crowd that understands the least.
articles.body:
This week, tens of thousands of Americans packed stadiums and living rooms to watch the U.S. beat Bosnia-Herzegovina 2–0 and advance to the Round of 16 at the World Cup. If you've ever stood in a crowd like that — the wall of sound, the strangers hugging, the chant that starts in one corner and swallows the whole stadium — you know there is almost nothing on earth like it. A crowd with one voice is one of the most powerful forces humans can generate.

Which is exactly why the story in Acts 19 should make us shiver a little.

Luke, the careful historian who wrote Acts, records a scene in the city of Ephesus that reads like a news report from our own century. A silversmith named Demetrius called a meeting of his trade guild. His business — crafting silver shrines of the goddess Artemis — was in trouble, and he named the threat plainly: "This fellow Paul has convinced and led astray large numbers of people... He says that gods made by human hands are no gods at all" (Acts 19:26, NIV).

Notice what Demetrius did next, because it's a move as old as commerce and as fresh as this morning's outrage cycle. He didn't say, My income is threatened. He wrapped his wallet in a flag and a temple: "There is danger... that the temple of the great goddess Artemis will be discredited" (v. 27). Self-interest never markets itself as self-interest. It always dresses up as something noble.

It worked. The city erupted. The mob seized two of Paul's traveling companions and rushed into the great theater of Ephesus — a stone arena that held some 25,000 people, and whose ruins you can still stand in today.

And then Luke writes one of the most quietly devastating sentences in the New Testament:

"The assembly was in confusion: Some were shouting one thing, some another. Most of the people did not even know why they were there." (Acts 19:32)

Read that again. Twenty-five thousand voices at full volume — and most of them had no idea what the meeting was about. When a man named Alexander tried to speak, the crowd drowned him out by chanting "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!" — for two hours (v. 34).

Two hours. Stand up right now and try shouting one sentence for two minutes. The Ephesians kept it up for a hundred and twenty. That is the strange physics of a crowd: fervor rises precisely as understanding falls.

Two thousand years before social media, Luke diagnosed the algorithm. A pile-on needs only three ingredients: someone whose interests are threatened, a sacred-sounding cause to hide those interests behind, and a mass of people who joined the noise without ever learning what started it. We've watched this exact story play out on our phones — the quote-tweets, the comment wars, the reputations torn down by thousands of people who, if you stopped them mid-shout, could not tell you what the accused person actually said. The theater at Ephesus never closed. It just went online.

Now, here is where the story turns — and where God hides a lesson so understated it's easy to miss.

The riot was not calmed by an apostle. Paul wanted to go in and address the crowd — you have to love his courage — but the disciples and even some friendly provincial officials begged him not to (vv. 30–31). Instead, God's instrument that day was an unnamed city clerk, a mid-level civic bureaucrat, who finally quieted the mob with two arguments so calm they feel almost boring: These men have committed no actual crime, and if we have a grievance, the courts are open (vv. 37–38).

In a chapter full of shouting, the man God used was the one who lowered the temperature. There's a whole theology of public life in that detail. We tend to believe the Kingdom advances through the loudest voice in the arena. Acts 19 suggests it more often advances when somebody — anybody — refuses to match the crowd's volume and simply tells the truth at a normal decibel level.

And notice what the clerk conceded without meaning to. He reassured the crowd that Artemis's greatness was beyond dispute — the whole world knew her temple, one of the Seven Wonders of the ancient world. Yet within a few centuries, the temple of Artemis was rubble. Today you can visit its site in Turkey: a single reconstructed column standing in a marshy field. Meanwhile, the message preached by the tentmaker they wanted to lynch is confessed by billions of people on every continent — including, in all likelihood, you.

Demetrius was right about one thing: the gospel really was bad for the idol business. It still is. Anything hand-made that we treat as ultimate — a portfolio, a platform, a physique, a political tribe — the gospel quietly discredits, and the part of us invested in the idol will riot every time. When you feel that inner Demetrius rising, that defensive fury when something you've built your security on gets questioned, it's worth asking his question honestly: Is my god in danger — or just my income? A god who can be endangered by a tentmaker was never a god at all.

Here's the encouraging part, and it's not small. You and I live in an age of theaters — arenas of noise where fervor substitutes for understanding, where two-hour chants have been upgraded to two-week news cycles. It is easy to feel that the truth doesn't stand a chance against that much volume. But Luke wants you to see the arithmetic of Ephesus: the mob had 25,000 voices and two hours of stamina. The gospel had a handful of frightened friends and a God who cannot be discredited. Ask the marsh in Turkey which one lasted.

So this weekend, when the crowds roar — at the World Cup, on your feeds, wherever — enjoy the human spectacle. Cheer for the goals. But keep this in your pocket: never confuse the volume of a crowd with the truth of its cause, and never underestimate what God can do with one calm voice in a screaming arena. The men who started the riot are remembered only because of the man they rioted against.

articles.title: The Night Asaph Couldn't Sleep
articles.dek: Psalm 77 was written at 3 a.m. by a man asking whether God had quit on him. What he did next is the oldest — and best — anxiety treatment on record.
articles.quote: Asaph never got an answer to his questions. He got something better: a bigger question.
articles.body:
With 165 million Americans under heat warnings this holiday weekend, a lot of us will find out tonight what it's like to lie awake at 3 a.m. — sheets kicked off, mind racing, staring at a ceiling fan that isn't helping. There is something about the sleepless hours that turns every worry into a verdict. Doubts that seem manageable at noon feel like certainties at 3 a.m.

Which is exactly the hour Psalm 77 was written.

Asaph — a worship leader, of all people, the man whose job was to help Israel sing — tells us plainly: "At night I stretched out untiring hands, and I would not be comforted" (v. 2). And then this professional worshiper does something that should forever change how you think about what's allowed in prayer. He asks God six questions that most of us would be afraid to say out loud in church:

"Will the Lord reject forever? Will he never show his favor again? Has his unfailing love vanished forever? Has his promise failed for all time? Has God forgotten to be merciful? Has he in anger withheld his compassion?" (Psalm 77:7–9)

Sit with how staggering it is that these verses exist. This is Scripture — God's own book — and God chose to include a chapter in which a believer asks, essentially, Has God's unfailing love failed? The Bible put a man's darkest doubts into its official songbook. Whatever else that means, it means this: God is not scandalized by your 3 a.m. questions. He heard Asaph's, and instead of striking him down, He published them.

But the psalm doesn't end in the questions, and the way out is so practical you could write it on an index card.

Verse 10 is the hinge of the whole psalm — the exact moment a drowning man's foot touches bottom: "Then I thought..." Asaph makes a decision. Not a feeling — his feelings were still wrecked. A decision: "I will remember the deeds of the LORD; yes, I will remember your miracles of long ago. I will consider all your works and meditate on all your mighty deeds" (vv. 11–12).

Count the first-person verbs: I will remember. I will remember. I will consider. I will meditate. Four hammer blows of deliberate memory. Asaph grabs his own wandering mind by the collar and marches it back through history — to the Exodus, to the God "who performs miracles," who "with your mighty arm... redeemed your people" (vv. 14–15).

Here's what modern clinicians would notice: Asaph is doing, three thousand years early, what therapists now call cognitive reframing — deliberately replacing a runaway thought spiral with evidence. Anxiety always argues from an empty future: What if? What if? What if? Asaph answers from a full past: But He did. He did. He did. You cannot fight a feeling with a feeling. You can fight a feeling with a memory.

This week, rescue crews in Venezuela are still working the earthquake rubble a full week after the ground stopped shaking — pulling, as one report put it, on "a thread of hope" that someone might yet be found alive. Nobody digs like that for seven days on optimism. They dig because it has happened before — because rescuers remember the survivors who came out of other ruins on day eight and day nine. Memory is what keeps hope's hands moving when feelings have long since quit. That is precisely the muscle Asaph is training in this psalm.

And notice the subtle miracle of grammar as the psalm turns. In verses 7–9, the subject of nearly every sentence is Asaph's crisis. From verse 11 onward, the subject of every sentence is God. Your ways... Your works... Your mighty arm... You redeemed. Asaph never actually receives an answer to his six questions. God never appears and explains Himself. What happens instead is that Asaph's questions get crowded out by a bigger one: "What god is as great as our God?" (v. 13). He walks into the psalm interrogating God and walks out lost in wonder — not because his circumstances changed, but because his gaze did.

That's the fresh word hiding in this old psalm: faith at 3 a.m. is not the absence of questions; it is the discipline of remembering in the dark what God showed you in the light.

So here is tonight's assignment, whether the heat keeps you up or your heart does. Before you reach for the phone — the phone always loses — reach backward instead. Name three of them. The prayer God answered when you were twenty-five. The provision that arrived the week you'd given up. The person He sent you the year everything caved in. Say them out loud if you have to; Asaph did — this psalm was set to music and performed. Your history with God is not a scrapbook. It is ammunition.

And if you're in a season where the questions are winning — where it genuinely feels like His unfailing love has vanished — take heart from the man who wrote those very words. He put them in a song. The song made it into the Bible. And three thousand years of sleepless believers have found their way home by it, one deliberate memory at a time. The night is real. But so is the track record.

articles.title: The War That Started With a Sympathy Card
articles.dek: David sent condolences. The grieving king's advisers called it espionage — and tens of thousands died for their suspicion. 1 Chronicles 19 is the Bible's masterclass on the price of assuming the worst.
articles.quote: Suspicion feels like intelligence. It is usually just fear wearing glasses.
articles.body:
Every counselor will tell you the same thing: the most damaging sentence in any marriage, family, or church is almost never an insult. It's an interpretation. He didn't text back — he's ignoring me. She questioned my numbers in the meeting — she's after my job. We are meaning-making machines, and when the data is ambiguous, fear is usually the fastest writer in the room.

If you want to see where that road ends, 1 Chronicles 19 is the Bible's cautionary tale — and it begins, of all places, with a sympathy card.

The king of the Ammonites died. David, remembering that the old king had once shown him kindness, did something genuinely warm-hearted: he sent a delegation to the son, Hanun, "to express sympathy to him concerning his father" (v. 2). No agenda. No treaty demands. Just a neighboring king saying, I'm sorry about your dad.

Then the advisers got hold of it.

"Do you think David is honoring your father by sending envoys to you to express sympathy? Haven't his envoys come to you only to explore and spy out the country and overthrow it?" (1 Chronicles 19:3)

Look closely at their evidence. There isn't any. Not one fact — only a question mark sharpened into a weapon: Do you really think he means it? Suspicion never needs proof; it only needs plausibility. And to a young, insecure king days into a job he inherited from a giant, plausible was enough.

So Hanun took David's grief counselors and humiliated them in the most calculated way an ancient Near Eastern mind could devise: he shaved their beards and cut their garments off at the buttocks, then sent them home (v. 4). In that culture, a man's beard was his dignity — his signature. This was not a prank. It was a public unmanning, designed to make David's kindness look like weakness.

And here the story could have ended. One insult, absorbed. Instead, Ammon's court did what the suspicious always do next: having wronged someone, they assumed retaliation was coming — the guilty mind projects — and hired 32,000 chariots from the Arameans (vv. 6–7). Mercenaries hired, armies mustered, and by the chapter's end tens of thousands of soldiers were dead and an empire's border had been redrawn — all of it traceable to a single poisoned interpretation of a sympathy card. Verse 6 contains the quiet epitaph: the Ammonites realized they had made themselves "a stench in David's nostrils." Suspicion promised to protect Hanun's kingdom. It cost him the kingdom instead.

Two details in this chapter, though, are pure grace — easy to skim, impossible to forget once you see them.

The first is what David did for his humiliated men. He didn't order them back to Jerusalem to be debriefed in their disgrace. Word was sent: "Stay at Jericho till your beards have grown, and then come back" (v. 5). Think about what that is: a king giving shamed men time and distance to heal before they had to face the capital. He didn't tell them to tough it out. He didn't pretend nothing had happened. He built a shelter for their dignity. Somewhere in your world is a person freshly humiliated — laid off, divorced, publicly corrected — and 1 Chronicles 19:5 is your template: protect their healing, don't rush their re-entry. It's what God does with us; Scripture is full of wilderness years that were really just Jericho — grace periods for beards to grow back.

The second is Joab's battle cry. Caught between two armies because of someone else's paranoia — a war he never chose, on terrain he didn't pick — David's general says one of the most quietly magnificent sentences in the Old Testament:

"Be strong, and let us fight bravely for our people and the cities of our God. The LORD will do what is good in his sight." (1 Chronicles 19:13)

Do your duty; trust God with the outcome. No bravado, no guarantee of victory claimed — just full effort and open hands. It's the exact posture for anyone walking into a battle they didn't start: the custody hearing, the restructuring, the diagnosis. Fight bravely; the results belong to the Lord.

But the lasting gift of this chapter is its warning, and we need it more than Hanun did, because we now process more ambiguous signals in a day than he saw in a year. Every unanswered email, every terse text, every meeting we weren't invited to arrives as a Rorschach blot, and somewhere inside each of us sits a council of advisers whispering, Do you really think they meant well? The advisers always sound smart. Cynicism always sounds smart. That's its trick — suspicion feels like intelligence, when it is usually just fear wearing glasses.

Here's the discipline that breaks the cycle, and it costs nothing: when the evidence is ambiguous, extend the interpretation you'd want extended to you. Paul gives the New Testament version in one clause — love "always trusts" (1 Corinthians 13:7) — not gullibility, but a settled policy of reading people generously until facts, not fears, say otherwise. Generous interpretation costs you a little pride when you're occasionally wrong. Suspicion costs you wars, marriages, churches, and thirty years of Ammonite-Israelite bloodshed.

David sent kindness and it was read as conspiracy — and if that's ever happened to you, notice that the chapter never blames David for the sympathy card. Heaven's accounting credited the kindness, not the misreading of it. Keep sending the card. How it's received is Hanun's chapter. That you sent it is yours.

articles.title: "We'll All Share the Loot"
articles.dek: Proverbs opens its course on wisdom with a mugger's recruitment speech. Solomon knew something about temptation that we keep forgetting: it almost never advertises money. It advertises belonging.
articles.quote: Sin's real product has never been the loot. It's the word 'us.'
articles.body:
This week, a story made the rounds that restores your faith in strangers: a man named Sak Yiengjuntuek found a sunglasses case with $12,000 inside — more than two months' pay for many families — and simply gave it back. No hesitation worth reporting, no reward demanded. Just a quiet decision that money belonging to someone else was not his, even when no one was watching.

Three thousand years ago, Solomon opened the book of Proverbs with the photographic negative of that story — and where he placed it should get our attention.

Proverbs is the Bible's university of wisdom, and after a seven-verse preamble, lesson one is not about diligence, or speech, or money management. Lesson one is a mugger's recruitment pitch, quoted verbatim:

"My son, if sinful men entice you, do not give in to them. If they say, 'Come along with us; let's lie in wait for innocent blood... we will get all sorts of valuable things and fill our houses with plunder; cast lots with us; we will all share the loot' — my son, do not go along with them." (Proverbs 1:10–15, excerpts)

Why start here? Because Solomon is teaching his son — and us — to recognize the anatomy of every enticement we will ever face. Read the pitch slowly and count the pronouns. Come along with US. Cast lots with US. WE will all share. In five lines of sales copy, the gang mentions treasure twice — but togetherness five times. The loot is the excuse. The product is belonging.

That insight is worth the whole chapter, because it explains why smart people do dumb things. Almost nobody wakes up craving stolen goods. But everybody — everybody — wakes up craving a table where they're wanted. Throw in your lot with us is the most seductive sentence in any language, and it works on twelve-year-olds joining gangs, on traders at firms cooking the books, on employees who stay silent because the meeting room fell conveniently silent, and on church people who join a gossip circle because being inside the whisper feels so much warmer than being outside it. The bait varies; the hook is always fellowship. Sin recruits the lonely.

Notice, too, the fine print Solomon exposes a few verses later. The gang promised to ambush others, but "these men lie in wait for their own blood; they ambush only themselves!" (v. 18). Every enticement carries that same actuarial error: the trap you help set is the trap you eventually spring. Ask anyone who ever joined the office fraud, the affair, the pile-on. The loot was going to be shared; in the end, only the consequences were.

But if temptation's real currency is belonging, then the defense against it can't merely be willpower. White-knuckled refusal loses eventually, because you cannot starve a hunger into silence — the hunger for belonging is God-given and non-negotiable. It has to be fed somewhere. Which is exactly why Solomon frames the entire warning inside a family: "Listen, my son, to your father's instruction and do not forsake your mother's teaching. They are a garland to grace your head and a chain to adorn your neck" (vv. 8–9).

Don't rush past that image. A garland and a chain — in Solomon's world, those were honors draped on champions and royalty. He's making the counter-offer explicit: there is a table where you already belong, an identity already draped on you like a medal, and it was placed there by people who would take a loss to protect you. The young man who knows he's wearing the garland can hear "come along with us" and feel... nothing much. He's not for sale because he's not starving. The person most protected from counterfeit belonging is the one already rich in the real thing.

That's the deeper reading of Mr. Yiengjuntuek's twelve thousand dollars, and of every quiet act of integrity like it. When a man hands back a fortune nobody knew he'd found, he isn't proving he lacks desire. He's revealing that his identity is already funded from another account. Integrity is rarely the fruit of heroic resistance in the moment; it is the fruit of belonging settled long before the moment arrives.

This is where the proverb becomes gospel, because the family table Solomon describes is a preview of a bigger one. The whole Bible is the story of a Father whose instruction is a garland, not a cage — and of a Son who was, in the most literal sense, ambushed by sinful men for the loot of our redemption, so that outsiders could be adopted in. "See what great love the Father has lavished on us, that we should be called children of God — and that is what we are!" (1 John 3:1). Every enticement you'll face this week is, at bottom, an offer of membership. The Christian answer is not "I'm too strong for that." It is "I'm already taken."

So do two things before the weekend crowds gather. First, run Solomon's diagnostic on whatever is currently whispering to you — the scheme, the shortcut, the circle you know you shouldn't want into. Ignore what it's promising and ask only: what belonging is it selling? Name that, and the spell weakens on the spot. Second — and this is the part we skip — go feed someone else's hunger for the real thing. Somewhere near you is a person one lonely evening away from saying yes to the wrong "come along with us." A seat at your table this week might be the whole difference. It usually is. The gang in Proverbs 1 only ever recruited people nobody else had invited.
